11 min read
5 hours ago

--

Frontend teams should be able to add presence, live cursors, shared state, room events, and awareness without turning a product feature into an infrastructure project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most web applications start as single-player experiences.
A user logs in.
They open a dashboard.
They edit a document.
They update a form.
They move through the product alone.
Then the product matures.
Teams want to collaborate inside the same workspace.
Managers want to see who is viewing a report.
Editors want to know who is changing a document.
Design tools need live cursors.
Admin panels need activity indicators.
SaaS dashboards need shared context.
Internal tools need multiple people working in the same view without overwriting each other.
At that point, the product starts becoming multiplayer.
The first instinct is usually simple:
“Let’s add WebSockets.”
That sounds reasonable. WebSockets make it possible to create a two-way interactive communication session between the browser and a server. The WebSocket protocol itself is designed for two-way communication between a client and a remote host, layered over an opening handshake and message framing.
But WebSockets are only transport.
They are not collaboration.
That difference matters.

Transport Is Not the Same as Collaboration
A realtime transport layer answers a narrow question:
“How do messages move between the browser and the server?”
A collaboration layer answers a broader set of questions:

• Who is in this workspace right now?

• Which users should see each other?

• What state should be shared?

• What state should remain local?

• What happens when a user disconnects?

• What happens when they reconnect?

• How do we remove stale cursors?

• How do we handle multiple tabs?

• How do we prevent two users from editing the same thing blindly?

• How do we keep the UI understandable when many users are active?
These are not WebSocket questions.
They are product architecture questions.
A WebSocket connection gives you a pipe. It does not give you rooms, presence, awareness, shared state, cursor behavior, permissions, lifecycle handling, or framework integration.
That is why many teams underestimate the work.
They think they are adding a small collaborative feature.
In reality, they are starting to build realtime infrastructure.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Transport Is Not the Same as Collaboration.
The Backend Work Appears Gradually
Collaboration work rarely arrives all at once.
It usually starts with one feature.
For example:
“Show who is currently viewing this page.”
At first, the implementation seems manageable.
You create a connection.
You send a join event.
You broadcast a list of users.
You remove users when they leave.
Then the edge cases appear.

• What if the user closes the laptop?

• What if the tab sleeps?

• What if the browser does not send a clean disconnect event?

• What if the network drops for six seconds?

• What if the same user opens three tabs?

• What if they switch workspaces without leaving the previous room correctly?

• What if presence updates arrive out of order?

• What if the server restarts?

• What if the user has permission to view the dashboard but not the document inside it?
This is where the backend grows.
Not because the first feature was complicated, but because collaboration has lifecycle.
Once lifecycle appears, the system needs structure.

The Minimum Backend for Collaboration Is Larger Than It Looks
A basic collaboration backend often needs more than a WebSocket endpoint.
It may need:
connection management
room membership
peer identity
presence state
heartbeat or liveness checks
disconnect cleanup
reconnect handling
message routing
authorization checks
state synchronization
event broadcasting
rate limiting
observability
horizontal scaling strategyEach of these has product implications.
For example, room membership is not just a technical list of socket IDs. It defines who belongs together inside the same collaborative context.
Presence is not just “online” or “offline.” It tells users whether another person is viewing, editing, typing, selecting, presenting, or idle.
Shared state is not just a JSON object. It requires decisions about conflict behavior, merge strategy, persistence, and ownership.
Live cursors are not just coordinates. They require throttling, cleanup, viewport mapping, identity, and visibility rules.
This is why collaboration features often become infrastructure work.
The UI looks small.
The system behind it is not.

Frontend Teams Should Not Have to Own All of This
There are cases where building a custom realtime backend is the right decision.
Large companies may have strict requirements around scaling, compliance, observability, persistence, and internal infrastructure. Some products are realtime-first by nature: multiplayer games, video conferencing platforms, trading systems, collaborative document editors, and large-scale whiteboards.
Those teams may need deep control over transport and backend architecture.
But many SaaS products do not start there.
They need common collaboration primitives:
rooms
presence
awareness
live cursors
shared state
events
locks
comments
viewport sync
history
framework bindingsThey do not necessarily need to build the entire realtime foundation from scratch.
For many frontend teams, the better question is not:
“How do we build a realtime backend?”
The better question is:
“What collaboration experience are we trying to add?”
That shift changes the architecture.
Instead of starting with servers, sockets, and message routing, teams can start with the user experience:

• Can users see who is present?

• Can they understand who is editing?

• Can they avoid stepping on each other’s work?

• Can they point, comment, select, or follow each other?

• Can collaboration be added gradually without rewriting the app?
That is usually the right level of thinking for product teams.

Collaboration Should Be Composable
Good collaboration features are not one giant feature.
They are composed from smaller primitives.
For example, a collaborative dashboard might need:
room: dashboard:revenue
presence: who is viewing
awareness: who is focused on which chart
events: user highlighted a metric
viewport sync: presenter is guiding others
comments: discussion on a specific chartA collaborative kanban board might need:
room: board:engineering-roadmap
presence: who is active
live cursors: where users are pointing
locks: who is editing a card
shared state: card positions
events: card moved or comment addedA collaborative form might need:
room: form:customer-application-123
presence: who is reviewing
awareness: focused field
locks: prevent simultaneous editing
comments: field-level discussion
history: review changesThe primitives are similar.
The product experience is different.
That is why composability matters.
A collaboration layer should not force every product into the same model. It should provide reusable building blocks so teams can design the right experience for their use case.

The “Just Add WebSockets” Trap
WebSockets are useful.
They are also low-level.
Using WebSockets directly means you still need to design the protocol between clients and the server.
For example, your app may start with messages like this:
{
"type": "join-room",
"roomId": "dashboard:revenue",
"userId": "user_123"
}Then you add presence:
{
"type": "presence-update",
"roomId": "dashboard:revenue",
"userId": "user_123",
"status": "viewing"
}Then cursors:
{
"type": "cursor-move",
"roomId": "dashboard:revenue",
"userId": "user_123",
"x": 428,
"y": 192
}Then state:
{
"type": "state-update",
"roomId": "dashboard:revenue",
"key": "selectedRange",
"value": "last_30_days"
}Then events:
{
"type": "event",
"roomId": "dashboard:revenue",
"event": "metric-highlighted",
"payload": {
"metricId": "mrr"
}
}This works in a prototype.
But soon you need validation, versioning, permissions, cleanup, retries, ordering rules, merge behavior, testing, and debugging.
You are no longer just using WebSockets.
You are designing a collaboration protocol.
That may be acceptable if collaboration is the core of your company.
But if collaboration is a feature inside a broader product, this is usually too much infrastructure work for the frontend team.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Just Add WebSockets Trap.
Not Every Realtime Feature Needs the Same Transport
Another mistake is assuming every realtime feature needs the same technical approach.
Some features need bidirectional communication.
Examples:
live cursors
collaborative editing
room events
shared state
presence updatesOther features may only need server-to-client updates.
Examples:
notifications
system status
progress updates
read-only dashboard refreshes
activity feedServer-Sent Events, for example, are designed as a one-way connection where the server can send events to the client, but the client does not send events back over the same channel.
That distinction matters.
Realtime architecture should be chosen based on the product need.
Collaboration usually requires more than “server pushes updates to the browser.” It often needs users to send activity, presence, interactions, cursor movement, state changes, and temporary signals back into the room.
This is why collaboration features usually benefit from a higher-level abstraction.
The important decision is not only WebSocket vs SSE vs polling.
The important decision is:
“What primitives does this product need to feel collaborative?”

The Real Bottleneck Is Not Always Backend Capacity
When teams discuss realtime collaboration, they often focus on backend capacity.

• How many connections can we support?

• How do we scale the WebSocket server?

• How do we broadcast efficiently?

• How do we use Redis or another coordination layer?

• How do we deploy this reliably?
These are valid questions.
But the more immediate bottleneck is often product engineering complexity.
The frontend team needs APIs that feel natural.
For example:
const room = createRoom("dashboard:revenue");room.presence.update({
status: "viewing",
currentSection: "MRR"
});room.events.send("metric-highlighted", {
metricId: "mrr"
});This kind of API lets developers think in product terms.
The alternative is making every product team think in transport messages, socket IDs, room maps, heartbeat intervals, and cleanup logic.
That creates friction.
Good developer experience matters because collaboration features are already complex enough at the UX level.
The infrastructure should not make them harder.

Framework Integration Matters
Modern frontend teams do not build in raw JavaScript alone.
They build with React, Vue, Svelte, Solid, Angular, Next.js, and other frameworks.
A collaboration layer should fit the way those frameworks manage state and rendering.
React developers expect hooks and providers.
Vue developers expect plugins and composables.
Svelte developers expect stores and actions.
Solid developers expect signals.
Angular developers expect dependency injection and services.
Next.js developers need server-side patterns for authentication and token generation.
Without framework integration, teams often create their own wrappers.
That leads to duplicated code and inconsistent patterns.
A good collaboration SDK should make the common path easy in each framework while keeping the core model consistent.
This is especially important for open-source adoption.
Developers are more likely to try a tool if the integration feels native to their stack.

Collaboration Also Needs Design Rules
Technical primitives are necessary, but they are not enough.
A product can have perfect realtime infrastructure and still feel chaotic.
For example:

• Too many cursors can distract users.

• Too many presence indicators can create noise.

• Typing indicators can feel unnecessary in some contexts.

• Locks can prevent conflicts but also slow users down.

• Comments can i
